title: Global: Health and Safety Fundamentals
subtitle: ISO 45001 and ILO Standards for Construction Workers
description: International health and safety principles for civil construction. Based on ISO 45001:2018 and ILO C167. Essential for workers moving between countries or working on international projects.

lessons.title: ISO 45001:2018 — OH&S Management Systems, Context and Leadership
lessons.content:
## ISO 45001:2018 — Occupational Health and Safety Management Systems

ISO 45001:2018 is the international standard for Occupational Health and Safety (OH&S) management systems. Published in March 2018, it replaced OHSAS 18001 and provides a universally applicable framework for organisations to prevent work-related injury and ill health.

## Why ISO 45001?

The International Labour Organization (ILO) estimates approximately **2.78 million workers** die from work-related accidents and diseases each year globally. ISO 45001 was developed to:
- Provide a single international OH&S standard applicable across industries and geographies
- Enable organisations to systematically improve OH&S performance
- Integrate OH&S into core business management using the Annex SL structure shared with ISO 9001 and ISO 14001
- Demonstrate commitment to workers, clients, regulators, and insurers

## High-Level Structure (Annex SL)

ISO 45001 uses the Annex SL structure common to modern ISO management standards:

| Clause | Title | PDCA Phase |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| 4 | Context of the Organisation | Plan |
| 5 | Leadership and Worker Participation | Plan |
| 6 | Planning | Plan |
| 7 | Support | Do |
| 8 | Operation | Do |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation | Check |
| 10 | Improvement | Act |

This structure allows integration with ISO 9001 (Quality) and ISO 14001 (Environment) into a single Integrated Management System (IMS).

## Clause 4 — Context of the Organisation

Organisations must understand their context before designing the OH&S management system:

**Internal factors (Clause 4.1):**
- Organisational culture, values, and management style
- Worker demographics, skills, and experience levels
- Past incident and near-miss history
- Existing OH&S programs and their effectiveness

**External factors (Clause 4.1):**
- Legal and regulatory requirements (HSWA, WHS Act, OSHA)
- Client and stakeholder OH&S expectations
- Industry best practices and codes of practice
- Economic, social, and competitive environment

**Interested parties (Clause 4.2):**
Identify who has a stake in the OH&S management system:
- Workers and their representatives (unions, HSRs, JHSCs)
- Contractors and subcontractors
- Clients and project owners
- Regulators (WorkSafe, OSHA, MOL)
- Community and neighbours
- Insurers and investors

**Scope (Clause 4.3):**
Define which sites, activities, workers, and time periods are covered by the OH&S management system.

## Clause 5 — Leadership and Worker Participation

**Top management leadership (Clause 5.1):**
Top management must personally demonstrate OH&S leadership — not just delegate to a safety manager:
- Take overall accountability — OH&S outcomes are a leadership responsibility
- Establish, implement and maintain an OH&S policy
- Ensure OH&S objectives are set and integrated into business strategy
- Allocate resources — people, time, money, technology
- Be visibly present on the floor and construction sites
- Reward and recognise safety behaviour — not just productivity

**OH&S Policy requirements (Clause 5.2):**
The OH&S policy must:
- Include a commitment to protect workers from injury and ill health
- Fulfil legal and other requirements
- Eliminate hazards and reduce OH&S risks
- Provide worker consultation and participation
- Commit to continual improvement
- Be communicated to workers and available to interested parties

**Worker Participation (Clause 5.4):**
Worker participation is a core ISO 45001 requirement — not optional:
- Workers must be consulted on hazard identification and risk assessment
- Workers involved in developing controls and safe work procedures
- Workers can raise concerns without fear of retaliation
- Workers have access to OH&S information relevant to their work
- Non-managerial workers participate in incident investigations

## Key Takeaways

- ISO 45001:2018 replaced OHSAS 18001 — 2.78 million work-related deaths annually drove its creation
- Annex SL structure: integrates with ISO 9001 and ISO 14001 for an Integrated Management System
- Context: understand internal factors, external factors, interested parties, and define scope
- Top management personal leadership: visible, accountable, resource-providing — not just delegation
- Worker participation: mandatory throughout — from hazard ID to incident investigation

questions.q: What international standard did ISO 45001:2018 replace?
options: ISO 9001
OHSAS 18001
questions.explanation: ISO 45001:2018 replaced OHSAS 18001 as the international OH&S management system standard. OHSAS 18001 was widely used but was a British standard — ISO 45001 elevated OH&S to a full international ISO standard.

questions.q: Which clause of ISO 45001 covers Leadership and Worker Participation?
options: Clause 4
Clause 5
Clause 6
Clause 8
questions.explanation: Clause 5 covers Leadership and Worker Participation — requiring top management to personally lead OH&S and ensuring genuine worker involvement in all aspects of the OH&S management system.

questions.q: In the ISO 45001 PDCA cycle, which clauses correspond to the 'Check' phase?
options: Clauses 4 and 5
Clauses 7 and 8
Clause 9
Clause 10
questions.explanation: Clause 9 (Performance Evaluation) corresponds to the 'Check' phase of PDCA — monitoring, measurement, internal audit, and management review. Clauses 4–6 = Plan, 7–8 = Do, 10 = Act.

questions.q: ISO 45001 Clause 4.2 requires identification of 'interested parties'. Which of the following is an interested party?
options: Only direct employees
Workers, contractors, clients, regulators, community, insurers, and unions
Only the PCBU and its officers
Only parties with a legal contractual relationship
questions.explanation: Interested parties include any person or organisation that can affect or be affected by the OH&S management system — workers, contractors, clients, regulators, community neighbours, insurers, investors, and worker representatives.

questions.q: Under ISO 45001 Clause 5.4, worker participation in OH&S is:
options: Optional — a best practice recommendation only
Mandatory — workers must be consulted on hazard ID, risk assessment, controls, and incident investigations
Required only for organisations seeking certification
Relevant only for workers in high-risk roles
questions.explanation: Worker participation is a core mandatory requirement under ISO 45001 Clause 5.4 — not optional. Workers must be consulted on hazard identification, risk assessment, development of controls, and incident investigations.

lessons.title: Hazard Identification, Risk Assessment and Hierarchy of Controls
lessons.content:
## ISO 45001 Clause 6 — Planning

Clause 6 is the planning heart of ISO 45001 — where organisations systematically identify hazards, assess risks AND opportunities, establish legal requirements, and set measurable OH&S objectives.

## Hazard Identification (Clause 6.1.2)

Hazard identification must be ongoing and comprehensive:

**Activities to consider:**
- Routine activities: daily work tasks performed regularly
- Non-routine activities: maintenance, shutdowns, start-ups, emergency response
- Infrastructure and equipment hazards
- Human and organisational factors: fatigue, stress, communication, inadequate training

**Management of change — triggers requiring new hazard identification:**
- New or modified work processes, equipment, or substances
- Changes to organisational structure or staffing
- New legislative requirements
- New knowledge about hazards emerging from research or incidents elsewhere

**Consulting workers during hazard identification:**
Workers performing the tasks are often the best source of hazard information. ISO 45001 requires their involvement — this is not optional consultation but mandatory participation.

## Risk Assessment

After identifying hazards, assess the level of OH&S risk:
**Risk = Likelihood × Severity**

| Risk Level | Example Scenario | Action Required |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Extreme | Unprotected worker in unstable excavation | Stop work immediately — redesign required |
| High | Worker on unguarded roof edge | Urgent controls — senior approval |
| Medium | Manual handling of 25 kg bags daily | Documented controls within 30 days |
| Low | Temporary noise from a single power tool | Standard precautions |

Risk assessments must account for:
- Existing controls and their actual effectiveness
- Vulnerable workers: new workers, young workers, pregnant workers, temporarily assigned
- Interactions between different work activities on the same site

## OH&S Opportunities (Clause 6.1.2.3)

ISO 45001 uniquely requires identifying **opportunities** to improve OH&S — not just managing risks:

**Examples of OH&S opportunities in civil construction:**
- New excavation technology (hydraulic shoring) eliminating manual timber shoring hazards
- Remote-controlled compaction equipment removing operator from vibration exposure
- Digital pre-start inspection apps improving reporting completeness and speed
- Prefabrication in a controlled yard reducing at-height work on site
- Sharing near-miss learnings across multiple projects simultaneously

## Hierarchy of Controls (Clause 8.1.2)

ISO 45001 mandates the application of the hierarchy of controls:

| Level | Control | Civil Construction Example |
|-------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Elimination** | Redesign task to remove the hazard entirely |
| 2 | **Substitution** | Replace hazardous chemical with safer alternative |
| 3 | **Engineering controls** | Trench shoring, machine guarding, noise enclosure |
| 4 | **Administrative controls** | Safe work procedures, permits, supervision, training |
| 5 | **PPE** | Hard hat, hi-vis, harness — last resort |

Multiple controls from different levels must be used simultaneously for high-risk activities. PPE alone is never acceptable for significant risks — it must supplement higher-level controls.

## Legal and Other Requirements (Clause 6.1.3)

Organisations must identify and have access to applicable legal and other requirements:

| Type | Examples for Civil Construction |
|------|---------------------------------|
| National legislation | HSWA 2015 (NZ), WHS Act 2011 (AU), OSHA 29 CFR (US) |
| Regulations | NZTA M/4 specification, CoPTTM, TNZ B/2 |
| Standards | ISO 45001, NZS 4203, AS 1576 |
| Contractual | Client project safety specifications |
| Industry codes | NZISM Safe Work Method Statements |

## OH&S Objectives (Clause 6.2)

OH&S objectives must be:
- **Measurable** (or evaluable)
- **Consistent** with the OH&S policy
- **Monitored** — progress tracked and reported
- **Communicated** to relevant workers
- **Updated** as conditions change

**Civil construction objective examples:**
- Zero Lost Time Injuries (LTIs) for the contract period
- 100% daily pre-start inspection completion rate
- All plant operators to hold current competency certificates
- LTIFR below industry benchmark by Q4
- 100% SWMS/SSSP completion before HRCW commences

## Key Takeaways

- Hazard identification: routine/non-routine work, human factors, change management — workers must be involved
- Risk = Likelihood × Severity — consider existing controls, vulnerable workers, and work interactions
- ISO 45001 uniquely requires identifying OH&S opportunities for improvement — proactive approach
- Hierarchy: Elimination first, PPE last — multiple levels for high-risk activities
- OH&S objectives: measurable, monitored, communicated, updated — not aspirational statements

questions.q: ISO 45001 Clause 6.1.2 requires hazard identification during 'management of change'. Which situation triggers this requirement?
options: Only when a fatal accident occurs
When new equipment, processes, substances, or organisational changes are introduced
Only during the annual OHS management review
Only for activities involving more than 10 workers
questions.explanation: Management of change triggers include: new or modified work processes, equipment, substances, organisational structure changes, new legislative requirements, and new knowledge about hazards. Any significant change requires fresh hazard identification.

questions.q: What makes ISO 45001 unique compared to earlier OH&S standards like OHSAS 18001?
options: It requires annual third-party certification
It requires organisations to identify OH&S opportunities for improvement, not just manage risks
It applies only to large organisations
It requires ISO 9001 certification first
questions.explanation: ISO 45001 Clause 6.1.2.3 uniquely requires identifying OH&S opportunities — new technology, process redesign, better systems, shared learning — alongside risk management. This proactive improvement focus was not explicitly required in OHSAS 18001.

questions.q: In the ISO 45001 hierarchy of controls, which level must always be considered first?
options: PPE — most immediately available
Administrative controls — easiest to implement
Elimination — remove the hazard entirely
Engineering controls — most reliable
questions.explanation: Elimination is always the first control to consider — can the hazard be removed entirely? Only when elimination is not reasonably practicable should lower levels be considered. Designing hazards out at source is always preferable to managing them.

questions.q: ISO 45001 OH&S objectives must be measurable, communicated, and monitored. Which of the following best represents a compliant objective?
options: 'We will try to improve safety on all our sites'
'Achieve zero Lost Time Injuries for the 2026 project and maintain 100% pre-start inspection completion'
'Safety is our top priority'
'All workers should work safely'
questions.explanation: Compliant objectives are specific and measurable — 'zero LTIs' and '100% pre-start completion' are measurable targets that can be monitored and evaluated. Vague statements like 'safety is our priority' do not meet Clause 6.2 requirements.

lessons.title: Resources, Competence, Awareness, Communication and Documented Information
lessons.content:
## ISO 45001 Clause 7 — Support

An effective OH&S management system requires adequate support infrastructure — competent people, sufficient resources, clear communication, and well-managed documented information.

## Resources (Clause 7.1)

Top management must provide resources necessary for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving the OH&S management system:
- **Human resources**: Qualified safety roles (HSOs, HSRs, JHSC members), competent supervisors
- **Infrastructure**: Inspection tools, monitoring equipment, first aid facilities
- **Time**: Paid time for workers to participate in OH&S activities, attend training
- **Financial resources**: Training, control implementation, equipment maintenance, PPE

Under-resourcing the OH&S system is itself an OH&S risk — and signals that leadership commitment is not genuine.

## Competence (Clause 7.2)

Organisations must:
1. Determine necessary competence for each OH&S-relevant role
2. Ensure workers have that competence through education, training, or experience
3. Identify gaps and take action to fill them
4. Retain documented evidence of competence

**Civil construction competency matrix example:**

| Role | Required Competencies |
|------|-----------------------|
| Excavator operator | NZQA US17285 / RIIMPO320F (AU), pre-start, exclusion zone management |
| Site supervisor | HSWA/WHS officer duties, first aid, plant management, incident investigation |
| Traffic management | CoPTTM STMS or TCP qualification, TMP preparation |
| Compaction tester | NDG licence (NZ), TNZ B/2 procedures, calibration records |
| Safety officer | OHS qualification, JHSC certification, incident investigation |

## Awareness (Clause 7.3)

Workers must be aware of:
- The OH&S policy and its commitments
- Their contribution to the OH&S management system (including personal benefits)
- The implications of not conforming to OH&S requirements
- Hazards, risks, and controls relevant to their specific work
- Incident investigation outcomes and lessons learned

Awareness is more than just training completion — workers must actively understand and apply what they know.

## Communication (Clause 7.4)

The organisation must establish processes for OH&S communication:

| Direction | Examples |
|-----------|---------|
| **Internal — downward** | Toolbox talks, pre-start meetings, notices, emails, safety bulletins |
| **Internal — upward** | Hazard reports, near-miss reports, worker suggestions, JHSC recommendations |
| **Internal — lateral** | Between crews, between shifts, across sites |
| **External — outward** | Communicating OH&S requirements to contractors, visitors, suppliers |
| **External — inward** | Receiving regulatory information, industry safety alerts, supplier SDSs |

Communication must be in a language and format workers understand — literacy and language barriers must be addressed.

## Documented Information (Clause 7.5)

ISO 45001 requires documented information to be maintained and retained:

**Maintained (kept current):**
- OH&S policy
- OH&S objectives
- Hazard identification and risk assessment records
- Legal requirements register
- Competency matrix and training records

**Retained (records as evidence):**
- Pre-start inspection records
- Incident investigation reports
- Toolbox talk sign-in sheets
- SWMS/SSSP completed and signed
- Audit and management review records
- Worker competency certificates

**Control of documented information:**
- Documents must be available where needed
- Protected from loss, improper use, or modification
- External documents (e.g. SDSs, standards) must be identified and controlled

## Key Takeaways

- Resources: top management must provide people, time, money, and tools — under-resourcing is itself an OH&S risk
- Competence: identify requirements per role, fill gaps, retain evidence
- Awareness: workers must understand policy, their contribution, consequences, and specific hazards
- Communication: all directions — upward, downward, lateral, and external — in understandable language
- Documented information: maintain current documents + retain records as evidence of OH&S system operation

questions.q: Under ISO 45001 Clause 7.2, what must an organisation do when it identifies a worker competency gap?
options: Document the gap and review it at the next annual meeting
Take action to fill the gap through training, education, or mentoring — and retain evidence
Reassign the worker immediately without further action
Report it to the regulator
questions.explanation: ISO 45001 Clause 7.2 requires taking action to fill competency gaps — through training, education, or experience. The organisation must also retain documented evidence of competence (training records, certificates, assessments).

questions.q: ISO 45001 Clause 7.3 requires workers to be 'aware' of OH&S matters. What does this include beyond training completion?
options: Signing the training attendance register
Actively understanding the OH&S policy, their personal contribution, consequences of non-conformance, and hazards specific to their work
Reading the company safety handbook once
Having a safety qualification on their personnel file
questions.explanation: Awareness goes beyond completing training — workers must actively understand: the OH&S policy commitments, their personal contribution to the system, consequences of non-conformance, and the specific hazards and controls relevant to their own work.

questions.q: A pre-start inspection record is an example of which type of documented information under ISO 45001 Clause 7.5?
options: Maintained documented information (kept current)
Retained documented information (records as evidence)
External documented information
Confidential documented information
questions.explanation: Pre-start inspection records are retained documented information — they serve as evidence that the OH&S system is operating. Maintained information (like the OH&S policy) is kept current. Retained information (like records) provides historical evidence.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes adequate OH&S communication under ISO 45001 Clause 7.4?
options: Monthly safety emails from management to all workers
Multi-directional communication — downward, upward, lateral, and external — in language workers understand
A notice board with safety posters in English only
Annual safety briefings at the company all-hands meeting
questions.explanation: ISO 45001 requires multi-directional communication — management to workers, workers to management, lateral between teams and shifts, and external communication with contractors and regulators. It must be in language and format workers can understand.

lessons.title: Management of Change and Emergency Preparedness
lessons.content:
## ISO 45001 Clause 8 — Operation

Clause 8 covers the operational implementation of OH&S controls — translating the plans and risk assessments from Clause 6 into actual workplace safety measures.

## Operational Planning and Control (Clause 8.1)

Organisations must plan, implement, control, and maintain processes to meet OH&S requirements:
- Establish criteria for processes (safe work procedures, permit-to-work systems)
- Implement controls in accordance with the hierarchy (Clause 8.1.2)
- Maintain documented information to demonstrate processes are carried out as planned

**Eliminating hazards and reducing risks (Clause 8.1.2):**
Apply controls in priority order:
1. Eliminate the hazard
2. Substitute with lower risk
3. Redesign work — engineering controls
4. Administrative controls (procedures, training, permits)
5. PPE

## Procurement (Clause 8.1.3)

Organisations must control procurement to ensure it does not introduce new OH&S risks:
- **Goods**: Equipment, plant, and materials must meet OH&S requirements before purchase/hire
- **Services**: Contractors and suppliers must meet OH&S requirements
- **Outsourcing**: OH&S requirements must flow through to outsourced activities

**Contractor management:**
- Communicate OH&S requirements to contractors before work begins
- Include OH&S criteria in tender and contract documents
- Verify contractor competency and compliance
- Co-ordinate OH&S where multiple contractors work in the same area

## Management of Change (Clause 8.1.3)

Changes to operations, processes, equipment, or organisation create new hazards. ISO 45001 requires a systematic process for managing change:

**Triggers for a change management review:**
- New or modified plant, equipment, or technology
- Changes to work processes or methods
- New chemical or hazardous substance
- Organisational restructure affecting supervision or staffing
- Legislative changes adding new requirements
- Incident investigation findings requiring process changes

**Change management process:**
1. Identify proposed change and scope
2. Assess OH&S risks of the change — before implementation
3. Determine controls required
4. Communicate to affected workers — update SWMS/SSSP
5. Train workers before the change takes effect
6. Implement and monitor
7. Review effectiveness after implementation

**Temporary changes** also require management of change — trial periods and temporary arrangements introduce hazards just as permanent changes do.

## Emergency Preparedness and Response (Clause 8.2)

Organisations must establish, implement, and maintain processes for emergency preparedness:

**Emergency preparedness requirements:**
- Identify potential emergency situations: fire, explosion, structural collapse, chemical spill, medical emergency, flood, electrical incident, confined space emergency
- Develop emergency response procedures for each scenario
- Test response procedures through drills and exercises
- Review and improve after drills and actual emergencies

**Emergency response plan must include:**
- Assembly points and evacuation routes
- Emergency contacts (emergency services, regulator, senior management, next of kin protocols)
- First aid resources and trained first aiders (ratio requirements vary by jurisdiction)
- Emergency communication methods (radio, phone, alarm systems)
- Roles and responsibilities during an emergency
- Site-specific considerations (underground services, confined spaces, hazardous materials)

**Drills:**
- Conducted at appropriate frequency — typically annually for full evacuation
- All workers must participate
- Results documented — deficiencies corrected
- New workers inducted on emergency procedures before starting work

## Key Takeaways

- Operational controls: safe work procedures, permit-to-work, apply hierarchy of controls
- Procurement: OH&S requirements flow to equipment, contractors, and outsourced activities
- Management of change: assess risks BEFORE any change — temporary changes also require review
- Emergency preparedness: identify scenarios, write procedures, conduct drills, document outcomes
- Emergency plans must be tested — drills reveal gaps that paperwork cannot

questions.q: Under ISO 45001 Clause 8.1.3, which of the following requires a change management OH&S review?
options: Annual safety policy review
Introduction of a new chemical, piece of equipment, or change to work process
Routine replacement of worn PPE
Change of safety officer
questions.explanation: Management of change applies to new plant, equipment, technology, work processes, chemicals, organisational restructures, and legislative changes. These all introduce potential new hazards requiring assessment BEFORE implementation.

questions.q: ISO 45001 Clause 8.1.3 requires OH&S requirements to be communicated to contractors. When must this occur?
options: After the contractor has started work
Before the contractor begins work
During the first JHSC meeting after mobilisation
At the first incident investigation
questions.explanation: OH&S requirements must be communicated to contractors BEFORE work begins — included in tender documents, contract terms, and site induction. Contractors cannot comply with requirements they were not informed of in advance.

questions.q: How often should emergency response drills be conducted under ISO 45001?
options: Only when a new hazard is identified
At appropriate frequency — typically at least annually — with all workers participating
Every 5 years
Only for sites classified as high risk
questions.explanation: ISO 45001 requires emergency procedures to be tested at appropriate frequency. For most civil construction sites this means at least annual full evacuation drills. All workers must participate, and results must be documented with deficiencies corrected.

questions.q: A temporary change to a work process is needed for a one-week trial. Does it require a management of change review?
options: No — management of change only applies to permanent changes
Yes — temporary changes introduce hazards just as permanent changes do and require pre-implementation assessment
Only if more than 5 workers are affected
Only if the change involves new equipment
questions.explanation: Temporary changes require management of change review — hazards exist during the temporary period just as much as during permanent operations. ISO 45001 does not exempt temporary or trial changes from the change management process.

lessons.title: Audits, Incident Investigation and Continual Improvement
lessons.content:
## ISO 45001 Clauses 9 and 10 — Checking and Improving

The final two phases of the PDCA cycle — Check and Act — close the loop on the ISO 45001 management system. Without systematic evaluation and improvement, OH&S systems stagnate.

## Clause 9 — Performance Evaluation

**Monitoring, Measurement and Analysis (Clause 9.1):**
Organisations must monitor and measure OH&S performance:

**Leading indicators (proactive — predict safety):**
- Pre-start inspection completion rates
- Near-miss reports per month
- Toolbox talk attendance rates
- SWMS compliance audit scores
- Worker participation in hazard identification activities
- Training completion rates

**Lagging indicators (reactive — measure past failures):**
- Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)
- Total Recordable Injury Frequency Rate (TRIFR)
- Number of fatalities
- Number of regulatory notices and prosecutions
- Medical treatment injury rate

Best-practice OH&S performance management uses BOTH leading and lagging indicators — lagging indicators tell you what went wrong; leading indicators show whether your system is preventing future incidents.

## Compliance Evaluation (Clause 9.1.2)

Organisations must periodically evaluate compliance with legal and other requirements:
- Review against all applicable legislation (HSWA, WHS Act, OSHA, etc.)
- Review against contractual OH&S requirements
- Document outcomes and address any non-conformances
- Retain records of compliance evaluations

## Internal Audit (Clause 9.2)

Internal audits systematically evaluate whether the OH&S management system:
- Conforms to the organisation's own requirements and ISO 45001
- Is effectively implemented and maintained

**Audit program requirements:**
- Plan audits considering OH&S importance of the processes and areas
- Results of previous audits — re-audit areas with past non-conformances more frequently
- Auditors must be objective and impartial — cannot audit their own work
- Report audit results to management
- Retain as documented information

## Management Review (Clause 9.3)

Top management must review the OH&S management system at planned intervals:
- Review OH&S performance against objectives
- Review incident investigation outcomes
- Review audit results and compliance evaluations
- Consider changes in external/internal context
- Make decisions about resource allocation and improvements

## Clause 10 — Improvement

**Incident Investigation (Clause 10.2):**
All incidents, near-misses, and non-conformances must be investigated:

**Investigation objectives:**
1. Determine root causes (not just immediate causes)
2. Identify contributing factors
3. Determine if similar incidents could occur elsewhere
4. Develop corrective actions
5. Share learnings across the organisation

**Root cause analysis:** Go beyond the immediate cause ('the worker fell') to systemic causes ('no fall protection system, no competency verification, no SWMS for the task').

**Corrective Actions (Clause 10.2):**
Corrective actions must:
- Address root causes — not just symptoms
- Be tracked to completion with assigned responsibility and deadline
- Be verified as effective after implementation

**Continual Improvement (Clause 10.3):**
The organisation must continually improve the suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness of the OH&S management system. This is not about perfection — it is about systematic, ongoing improvement in OH&S performance year on year.

## Key Takeaways

- Leading indicators predict future safety; lagging indicators measure past failures — use both
- Internal audit: objective, impartial — cannot audit own work — report to management, retain records
- Management review: top management examines OH&S performance, incidents, audits, and resources
- Incident investigation: find ROOT CAUSES — not just immediate causes — share learnings
- Continual improvement (Clause 10.3): ongoing, systematic — the system must get better over time

questions.q: What is a 'leading indicator' in OH&S performance measurement?
options: Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)
Number of pre-start inspections completed — a proactive measure predicting future safety
Number of fatalities in the past year
Workers' compensation premium cost
questions.explanation: Leading indicators are proactive measures that predict future safety performance — pre-start completion rates, near-miss reports, training completion, toolbox talk attendance. Lagging indicators (LTIFR, fatalities) measure past failures after they have already occurred.

questions.q: Under ISO 45001 Clause 9.2, who can conduct an internal audit?
options: Any manager can audit their own team
Objective and impartial auditors — must not audit their own work
Only external ISO certification auditors
Only the health and safety officer
questions.explanation: Internal auditors must be objective and impartial — they cannot audit their own work. This independence is essential for credible audit findings. Auditors can be internal staff as long as they are auditing functions other than their own.

questions.q: An incident investigation reveals a worker fell from an unguarded edge. The immediate cause is identified as 'unguarded edge'. ISO 45001 requires finding:
options: The immediate cause only — and install a guardrail
Root causes — why was the edge unguarded, was there no SWMS, no supervision, no competency check?
Who was at fault — disciplinary action
The cost of the incident for insurance purposes
questions.explanation: ISO 45001 requires finding ROOT CAUSES — not just the immediate cause. Why was the edge unguarded? Was there no SWMS? No competency verification? No supervision? Addressing only the immediate cause prevents recurrence only of the exact same scenario.

questions.q: ISO 45001 Clause 10.3 requires continual improvement. What does this mean in practice?
options: Achieving zero injuries immediately
Systematic, ongoing improvement in the suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness of the OH&S management system over time
Changing the OH&S policy every year
Implementing a new safety initiative each quarter
questions.explanation: Continual improvement means systematic, ongoing improvement in OH&S performance year on year — not perfection overnight. It involves using audit findings, incident learnings, management reviews, and worker suggestions to progressively enhance the system.

questions.q: ISO 45001 Clause 9.1.2 requires compliance evaluation. What must be documented?
options: Only non-conformances with legal requirements
Results of compliance evaluations including any non-conformances found and corrective actions taken
Only the legal requirements that apply
An annual letter from the regulator confirming compliance
questions.explanation: Compliance evaluation results must be documented — including which requirements were evaluated, the findings, any non-conformances identified, and corrective actions taken. These records demonstrate the organisation is actively monitoring its legal compliance.
